title: A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware
summary: The repository XML is the pinned General Corpus copy of University of Michigan CME item tenwives, a 2003 encoding of Frederick J. Furnivall’s 1871 edition, pages 29–33. Furnivall transcribed the unique known witness, National Library of Wales Brogyntyn MS II.1 (formerly Porkington MS 10), fols. 56v–59v, but supplied bracketed line 8 to regularize a six-line stanza and supplied the now-standard title for an untitled manuscript opening. A complete 425-canvas NLW IIIF sequence of the manuscript is publicly available under the Public Domain Mark and cached locally as a 99,025,312-byte bundle; the poem maps to canvases 114–120. DIMEV record 3073, Salisbury’s 2002 METS edition, and Harris’s 2022 study are supporting descriptions or later editions, not ancestors of the U-M encoding. Current U-M No Copyright–United States labeling conflicts with the restrictive redistribution terms embedded in the 2003 XML and is kept as an unresolved provider-term question.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for tenwives
entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed by this repository’s conversion workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The pinned file is 11,176 bytes.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item tenwives
entities.description: A manually keyed and proofread 2003 electronic text of Furnivall’s title page, poem on pages 29–33, and work-level notes. It encodes 20 stanza groups and 120 line positions, including Furnivall’s supplied line 8, and omits the edition’s preface and other works.
notes: The header’s AUTHOR field contains a repository and shelfmark, not an intellectual author.
The PROFILEDESC declares Latin and includes an English-prose subject heading, but the encoded work is Middle English verse.

entities.label: A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware (abstract work)
entities.description: The anonymous Middle English alewife poem conventionally arranged as twenty six-line stanzas, distinguished from its sole witness, editorial titles, editions, encodings, and facsimiles.
notes: The schema has no abstract-work entity type; catalog_description is used to keep the intellectual work visible without making it the lineage primary subject.

entities.label: Frederick J. Furnivall, Jyl of Breyntford’s Testament … and Other Short Pieces (1871), pages 29–33
entities.description: The immediate printed source named by the U-M XML. Furnivall transcribed the poem from Porkington MS 10, supplied an editorial title and bracketed line 8, represented scribal curls and abbreviation expansions typographically, and added explanatory notes.

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Jyl of Breyntford’s Testament, by Robert Copland, Bokeprynter; The Wyll of the Deuyll and His Last Testament; A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware; A Balade or Two by Chaucer; and Other Short Pieces (London: printed for private circulation by Taylor and Co., 1871), pp. 29–33.
bibliographic.title: Jyl of Breyntford’s Testament … and Other Short Pieces
bibliographic.publisher: Printed for private circulation by Taylor and Co.

notes: Furnivall’s preface says Madden had already brought the work to public notice and Halliwell had quoted passages; 1871 is therefore treated as the first complete publication identified here, not the first mention or excerpt.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Furnivall’s complete 1871 volume
entities.description: A complete 64-page PDF scan of the 1871 volume. The target poem occupies printed pages 29–33, corresponding to PDF images 37–41.

entities.label: Internet Archive machine OCR of Furnivall’s complete 1871 volume
entities.description: Automatically generated plain-text OCR of the complete scan. It is useful for discovery of the preface and poem but is not authoritative for historical spelling, typography, punctuation, or supplied letters.

entities.label: National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.1, fols. 56v–59v
entities.description: The sole known textual witness of the poem within a 212-folio late-fifteenth-century English miscellany. The work begins without a title or rubric at the top of f. 56v and ends with Amen partway down f. 59v; the remainder of f. 59v begins an adjacent lover’s complaint.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.notes: A cataloged approximate witness date, not an exact composition year.

physical_description.extent: 212 folios plus flyleaves; poem on fols. 56v–59v
notes: The codex was copied by multiple scribes. The poem lies within the hand conventionally described as scribe L, whose run ends on f. 59v.
An inscription naming H. Hattun at f. 52v may identify the scribe of ff. 27r–59v or a patron; the current catalog does not resolve the role.
The manuscript has 119 written verse lines for this poem; Furnivall’s bracketed line 8 is absent.
The current detailed catalog supplies the title [The Tale of Ten Wives] and calls the text unique; the visible witness itself has no opening title.

entities.label: Complete NLW IIIF facsimile of Brogyntyn MS II.1 (object 4398349)
entities.description: The complete provider-published sequence from front cover through back cover, comprising 425 canvases. The target is canvases 114–120 (f. 56v–f. 59v; image identifiers 4398463–4398469). Source images report 4080 × 4080 pixels, while the public whole-image API caps delivered images at 1200 × 1200 pixels.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest with 425 color JPEG canvas images
notes: The Presentation Canvas URLs redirect to non-working paths as checked 2026-07-15. Exact Image API requests remain functional and are recorded for the work boundaries.
The locally cached complete bundle contains the provider manifest, exact-source inventory, and all 425 delivered images.

entities.label: National Library of Wales catalog description of Brogyntyn MS II.1
entities.description: The current collection-level and detailed contents description for the manuscript, including date, extent, scribal structure, access restrictions, target folios, supplied title, stanza form, edition reference, IMEV number, and surrogate information.

entities.label: Digital Index of Middle English Verse record 3073
entities.description: DIMEV v1.0 identifies IMEV/NIMEV 1852, gives the normalized incipit ‘Lief listeneth to me,’ titles the work The Ten Wives Tales, describes twenty six-line stanzas, supplies two subject terms, and records the Brogyntyn witness and Furnivall edition.

entities.label: Eve Salisbury, The Trials and Joys of Marriage (2002), A Talk of Ten Wives
entities.description: A later complete teaching edition with select bibliography, textual notes, glosses, and work classification. It records the manuscript and Furnivall edition, compares readings with the manuscript and Furnivall, and explicitly identifies line 8 as Furnivall’s presumed addition.

bibliographic.citation: Eve Salisbury, ed., The Trials and Joys of Marriage, Middle English Texts Series (Kalamazoo, MI: Medieval Institute Publications, 2002), ‘A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware,’ text and notes at printed pp. 95–101.
bibliographic.title: The Trials and Joys of Marriage

notes: The complete edition PDF maps this work’s text to PDF pages 96–99 and its select bibliography/notes to PDF pages 100–102.

entities.label: Carissa M. Harris, Cultivating Cummarship (2022)
entities.description: A modern scholarly chapter that identifies A Talk of Ten Wives as one of the earliest surviving alewife poems and analyzes its alehouse setting, female fellowship, sexual pedagogy, collective complaint, and misogynist framing.

bibliographic.citation: Carissa M. Harris, ‘Cultivating Cummarship: Female Friendship, Alcohol, and Pedagogical Community in the Alewife Poem,’ in Women’s Friendship in Medieval Literature, ed. Karma Lochrie and Usha Vishnuvajjala (Columbus: Ohio State University Press, 2022), pp. 157–176.
bibliographic.title: Cultivating Cummarship: Female Friendship, Alcohol, and Pedagogical Community in the Alewife Poem

rights.component: The medieval poem as an abstract textual work
rights.basis: The poem is attested in a manuscript currently dated circa 1470 and no modern authorship is involved in the underlying text.

rights.component: The current U-M digital item and encoding in the United States
rights.basis: The current U-M item displays the RightsStatements.org No Copyright–United States designation.
notes: The embedded 2003 availability statement separately prohibits redistribution by commercial and nonprofit content providers. This provider-term conflict is unresolved; NoC-US is not silently treated as cancellation of the embedded terms.
The designation is territorially limited to the United States.

rights.component: The redistribution terms embedded in the 2003 XML
rights.basis: The XML allows personal use, research, teaching, and linking with attribution but expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers.
notes: This is recorded as a provider-term layer, not as proof that U-M owns copyright in the medieval text or Furnivall edition.

rights.component: The 1871 edition, including Furnivall’s editorial contributions and published typography
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1871 and Furnivall died in 1910. The maximum U.S. term for such a published work and the ordinary UK life-plus-70 literary term expired long before 2026; UK edition-layout protection also lasts only 25 years from publication.

rights.component: The Internet Archive page scan and downloadable PDF
rights.basis: The scanned 1871 edition is public domain in the United States; no item-specific claim of new copyright in the faithful page images was found in the cached metadata.
notes: Internet Archive access and service terms remain separate from the copyright status of the underlying edition.

rights.basis: The OCR mechanically represents a public-domain 1871 volume and the item metadata provides no item-specific copyright or open-license statement for the OCR layer.

rights.component: The fifteenth-century manuscript text and historical writing
rights.basis: The witness is a fifteenth-century manuscript and the underlying work is medieval.

rights.component: The complete 425-canvas NLW digital facsimile and faithful manuscript images
rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest carries the Public Domain Mark handle, and NLW’s Public Domain Mark guidance says marked works may be copied, modified, distributed, and performed, including commercially, without permission.
notes: NLW recommends attribution and Europeana public-domain usage norms, but its page describes these as nonbinding guidelines rather than permission conditions.

rights.component: Metadata written by the National Library of Wales
rights.basis: NLW’s copyright guidance releases metadata written by NLW under CC0, while warning that third-party metadata may have different status.
notes: The catalog record itself was treated as NLW-authored, but the general policy’s third-party caveat should be rechecked for any separately contributed description.

rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 data, including record 3073 and its linked witness data
rights.basis: The Zenodo v1.0 release specifies Creative Commons Attribution 4.0.

rights.component: Salisbury’s modern edited text, notes, introduction, HTML presentation, and downloadable PDF
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and applicable copyright law
rights.basis: METS states that its digital editions are provided for individual use only and directs users to contact the Executive Director to reprint digital text. No open license was found for the PDF or rendered digital edition.
notes: METS separately licenses its XML under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. That statement is not applied to this cached PDF or to the rendered digital-edition layer.
The underlying medieval text and manuscript readings remain public domain.

rights.component: The 2022 book and Harris chapter in the Ohio State repository PDF
rights.basis: The repository item and exact cached license RDF identify Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International.
notes: The license permits sharing with attribution only for noncommercial purposes and prohibits distribution of adapted material. It is not suitable for unrestricted commercial repository redistribution.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The second stanza has five physically written lines in Brogyntyn MS II.1; no line occurs between ‘Sen we haue no othere songe’ and ‘Talys lett us tell.’
editorial_practices.representation: Furnivall inserts bracketed line 8, ‘For to singen us amonge,’ producing twenty regular six-line stanzas and 120 editorial line positions.
editorial_practices.description: The supplied line is an editorial intervention, not a lost line visible in the witness. Salisbury explicitly says it was presumably added by Furnivall to maintain the six-line stanza, and the IIIF image confirms its absence.
editorial_practices.scope: Furnivall page 29, line 8; U-M XML second LG

editorial_practices.source_feature: Scribal curls and abbreviated letter forms, including recurrent curls after final d, g, m, and n
editorial_practices.representation: Furnivall uses italic letters or letter groups for editorial expansions and comments that final curls may represent e; the U-M XML preserves many as HI1 REND='i' and distinguishes some superscript letters.
editorial_practices.description: This convention is central to comparing U-M italics with the manuscript. The explicit note supports curls read as final e, but not every italic sequence has been exhaustively mapped character by character to a single manuscript sign.
editorial_practices.scope: The poem on Furnivall pages 29–33 and corresponding U-M inline markup

editorial_practices.source_feature: The poem begins at the top of f. 56v with a large red initial and no title or rubric.
editorial_practices.representation: Furnivall supplies the printed heading ‘A Talk of Ten Wives on their Husbands’ Ware,’ which the U-M encoding carries as HEAD.
editorial_practices.description: The standard title is editorial and must not be described as a manuscript rubric.
editorial_practices.scope: Opening of the poem, Brogyntyn f. 56v / Furnivall p. 29

editorial_practices.source_feature: Furnivall’s complete 44-page edition with preface, contents, several works, and surrounding matter
editorial_practices.representation: The U-M item retains the edition title page, the complete five-page Ten Wives poem, and its work-level notes, while omitting Furnivall’s preface, contents, and other works.
editorial_practices.description: The encoding is complete for the selected poem but selective relative to Furnivall’s volume.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A large red initial at the untitled opening and red paragraph initials introducing later wives in the manuscript
editorial_practices.representation: The XML represents letters, capitalization, and some pilcrow characters but does not encode manuscript color or the decorated scale of the initials; it instead follows Furnivall’s supplied printed heading.
editorial_practices.description: Researchers studying layout, color, or work boundaries must consult the manuscript facsimile rather than infer rubrication from the XML.
editorial_practices.scope: Brogyntyn fols. 56v–59v and the corresponding XML poem

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/tenwives.xml, pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: Complete file; especially FILEDESC, AVAILABILITY, SOURCEDESC, ENCODINGDESC, PROFILEDESC, FRONT/TITLEPAGE, and BODY/DIV1
evidence.summary: The 11,176-byte file identifies AEH6189/tenwives/IMEV 1852, a 2003 encoding of Furnivall 1871 pages 29–33 transcribed from Brogyntyn II.1. It preserves the title page, poem, notes, five printed page breaks, seven folio milestones, italics/superscripts, the supplied bracketed line, and the legacy redistribution restriction, while also containing false Latin/prose cataloging fields.

evidence.citation: Direct structural and textual inspection of the pinned tenwives.xml artifact.
evidence.locator: FRONT/TITLEPAGE; BODY/DIV1; element counts and line sequence
evidence.summary: The file contains one poem, 20 LG elements, 120 L elements, five PB elements for pages 29–33, and seven manuscript-folio milestones from 56v to 59v. The second LG includes bracketed line 8, and inline HI1 markup records italic and superscript distinctions. The encoded content is Middle English verse, not Latin prose.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware, item tenwives.
evidence.locator: Item metadata, stable URL, collection production description, and rights display
evidence.summary: The current item identifies Furnivall as editor, 1871 publication, the stable ID tenwives, and the No Copyright–United States rights designation. Current collection documentation describes manual keying, in-house proofreading, and basic TEI encoding.
notes: The item’s MARC-style author display treats Brogyntyn 2.1 as an author; this is not accepted as intellectual authorship.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Jyl of Breyntford’s Testament … and Other Short Pieces (London, 1871), preface and pp. 29–33.
evidence.locator: Preface discussion of The Talk of Ten Wives; printed pages 29–33 / PDF images 37–41
evidence.summary: Furnivall names Porkington MS 10 as his source, prints the complete poem, supplies line 8 in brackets, and comments on final scribal curls and italic expansions. His preface states that Madden first brought the work to public notice and Halliwell quoted passages.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of the cached complete Furnivall 1871 PDF scan, target pages and title matter.
evidence.locator: PDF images 37–41; printed pages 29–33
evidence.summary: The scan confirms the supplied heading, the bracketed second-stanza line, italic editorial letters, manuscript folio callouts, stanza layout, and the poem’s complete page boundaries.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for item jylofbreyntfords00furn.
evidence.locator: Complete item metadata and file list
evidence.summary: The metadata identifies the complete public scan and exact PDF, DJVU, OCR, and metadata files for the 1871 volume.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive machine OCR of Furnivall’s complete 1871 volume.
evidence.locator: Preface around the Ten Wives discussion and OCR of printed pages 29–33
evidence.summary: The OCR exposes Furnivall’s discussion of Madden, Halliwell, manuscript identity, and the complete poem for searching. It is machine-generated and was checked against page images for material claims.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales Archives and Manuscripts, English miscellany / Brogyntyn MS II.1.
evidence.locator: Collection description, date, extent, access conditions, related descriptions, and digital-object link
evidence.summary: The current catalog identifies Brogyntyn MS II.1, former Porkington MS 10, dates it [c.1470], gives 212 folios plus flyleaves and multiple scribes, restricts access to the original, directs readers to surrogates, and links the public digital version.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales, detailed contents description for Brogyntyn MS II.1.
evidence.locator: Target entry at fols. 56v–59v; scribal hand L; surrogate list
evidence.summary: The detailed contents identify [The Tale of Ten Wives] at fols. 56v–59v as a unique text in six-line stanzas, cite Furnivall pages 29–33 and IMEV 1852, place it at the end of scribe L’s run, note the unresolved H. Hattun inscription, and list NLW Film 1046 and MEICRO Brogyntyn II.1.
notes: The PDF was publicly viewable in browser inspection but automated file retrieval returned 403 on 2026-07-15; no local cache path or checksum is asserted.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales, A Middle English Miscellany.
evidence.locator: Compilation place and dialect discussion
evidence.summary: NLW states that the manuscript’s exact compilation place is unknown and that Cheshire or Shropshire is consistent with the West Midland dialect of its English works.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS ii.1, IIIF Presentation 2 manifest, object 4398349.
evidence.locator: 425 canvases; target canvases 114–120 / image identifiers 4398463–4398469
evidence.summary: The manifest labels Brogyntyn MS ii.1, dates it [ca.1470], attributes the digital object to NLW, provides 425 canvases from front through back cover, and carries the Public Domain Mark handle. The complete sequence is cached with an exact inventory.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales IIIF Image API information for Brogyntyn MS II.1 f. 56v, image 4398463.
evidence.locator: width, height, Image API profile, maximum width/height, tiles, and sizes
evidence.summary: The source image reports 4080 × 4080 pixels and the profile sets maxWidth and maxHeight to 1200, explaining why a 1600-pixel whole-image request returns 1200 × 1200 pixels.

evidence.citation: Visual and inventory inspection of the complete cached NLW IIIF bundle for Brogyntyn MS II.1.
evidence.locator: Complete 425-image inventory; especially images/000114.jpg–images/000120.jpg, fols. 56v–59v
evidence.summary: All 425 inventory hashes and JPEGs were verified. Target inspection confirms an untitled opening with a large red initial, red paragraph initials, the physical absence of Furnivall line 8, continuous work coverage through Amen on f. 59v, and the adjacent complaint beginning on the remainder of that folio.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales, Public Domain Mark.
evidence.locator: Permitted reuse and nonbinding guidelines
evidence.summary: NLW says works marked with the Public Domain Mark may be copied, modified, distributed, and performed without permission, including commercially; attribution and Europeana usage guidance are described as nonbinding norms.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales, Copyright.
evidence.locator: Item-specific labels, faithful digitization, and metadata provisions
evidence.summary: NLW directs users to item-specific rights labels and releases metadata written by NLW under CC0, subject to caveats for third-party contributions.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, v1.0, record 3073, DOI 10.5281/zenodo.14585961.
evidence.locator: Records.xml record-3073; IMEV/NIMEV 1852; witness wit-3073-1
evidence.summary: DIMEV gives the normalized incipit, title The Ten Wives Tales, twenty six-line stanzas, the exact subjects ‘husbands, inadequacies in lovemaking’ and ‘women, as speakers/narrators,’ and one witness at Brogyntyn II.1, fols. 56v–59v, with Furnivall pages 29–33. The v1.0 data is CC BY 4.0.

evidence.citation: Eve Salisbury, ed., The Trials and Joys of Marriage (2002), A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware, Middle English Texts Series.
evidence.locator: Text detail, complete text, select bibliography, and notes
evidence.summary: METS identifies the work as Middle English poetry, fifteenth century, and Fabliau; records Brogyntyn II.1 fols. 56v–59v and Furnivall pages 29–33; cites Gossips’ Meeting as an alternate title; distinguishes manuscript and Furnivall readings; and states that line 8 was presumably added by Furnivall to maintain the six-line stanza.

evidence.citation: Rendered-page OCR and visual audit of the cached complete Trials and Joys of Marriage PDF.
evidence.locator: PDF pages 96–102 / printed pages 95–101
evidence.summary: The complete 277-page image-only PDF contains the target text on PDF pages 96–99 and its select bibliography and notes on PDF pages 100–102. The notes explicitly identify manuscript readings and Furnivall’s supplied line 8.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Copyright & Citation.
evidence.locator: Copyright section
evidence.summary: METS states that digital editions are provided for individual use only and that reprinting digital text requires contact with the Executive Director. It separately licenses XML under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

evidence.citation: Carissa M. Harris, Cultivating Cummarship: Female Friendship, Alcohol, and Pedagogical Community in the Alewife Poem, in Women’s Friendship in Medieval Literature (Ohio State University Press, 2022), pp. 157–176.
evidence.locator: Chapter 8, printed pp. 157–176 / PDF pp. 167–186; A Talk of Ten Wives especially pp. 160, 164–165
evidence.summary: Harris calls the work one of the earliest surviving alewife poems, dates it broadly 1453–1500, describes a Cheshire household-miscellany context, and analyzes the wives’ drinking, female fellowship, bawdy disclosure, sexual pedagogy, collective complaint, and misogynist framing.
notes: The current NLW catalog’s [c.1470] witness date and Cheshire-or-Shropshire uncertainty are preferred for physical-witness description; Harris’s broader dates and Cheshire formulation remain attributed scholarly claims.

evidence.citation: Ohio State University Knowledge Bank, Women’s Friendship in Medieval Literature, handle 1811/101697, item metadata and license RDF.
evidence.locator: Item title, editors, publisher, date, copyright statement, PDF bitstream, and license bitstream
evidence.summary: The official repository metadata identifies the 2022 Ohio State University Press volume, supplies the complete public PDF, records copyright, and provides an exact CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 license RDF.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.locator: Works first published or copyrighted before January 1, 1978
evidence.summary: The official duration guidance explains the maximum terms applicable to older published works; an 1871 publication is outside any subsisting U.S. term in 2026.

evidence.citation: UK Intellectual Property Office, How long copyright lasts.
evidence.locator: Written works and layout of published editions
evidence.summary: The official guidance gives the usual literary term as life plus 70 years and the layout term for published editions as 25 years from publication; Furnivall died in 1910 and his edition appeared in 1871.

open_questions.question: Does U-M consider the restrictive redistribution statement embedded in the 2003 XML superseded by the current No Copyright–United States label, and may the XML encoding and markup be redistributed in a commercial or nonprofit corpus?
next_steps: Request written clarification from U-M Library that separately addresses copyright status, provider terms, nonprofit redistribution, commercial redistribution, and reuse of the encoding/markup.
Until clarified, cite both the current NoC-US label and the embedded restriction rather than selecting whichever is more permissive.

open_questions.question: Can every italic letter or letter group in Furnivall pages 29–33 and the corresponding U-M HI1 markup be mapped to its exact manuscript abbreviation, curl, or other editorial rationale?
next_steps: Perform a character-by-character collation of Furnivall PDF images 37–41, U-M XML, and NLW IIIF canvases 114–120.
Distinguish explicit final-curl expansions, other abbreviation expansions, supplied letters, typographic emphasis, and OCR artifacts.
Record any consistent mapping in a future transcription-format study rather than generalizing from the first footnote alone.

open_questions.question: Does the H. Hattun inscription on f. 52v identify scribe L, a patron, or another associate of the manuscript?
next_steps: Consult current codicological and palaeographical scholarship on Brogyntyn MS II.1.
Do not convert the catalog’s alternatives into a named-scribe assertion without further evidence.

open_questions.question: What exact passages appeared in Halliwell before Furnivall’s 1871 complete edition, and did any other complete or partial publication precede it?
next_steps: Inspect Madden’s Syr Gawayne contents description and the relevant editions of Halliwell’s Dictionary of Archaic and Provincial Words.
Retain ‘first complete publication identified’ for Furnivall 1871 until the earlier publication record is fully inventoried.

review_notes: The repository artifact, U-M encoding, Furnivall edition, manuscript witness, Internet Archive scan/OCR, NLW IIIF facsimile, later METS edition, repertory data, and modern scholarship are separate entities with scoped relations.
The manuscript has no opening title or rubric. Furnivall’s title is editorial, even though it is now the preferred discovery title.
The conventional 120-line / twenty-six-line-stanza description includes Furnivall’s supplied bracketed line 8. The physical witness has 119 written verse lines.

The complete NLW facsimile bundle covers all 425 published canvases. Its filename retains the cache request profile ‘1600,’ but provider metadata caps whole-image delivery at 1200 × 1200 and every cached JPEG was verified at that delivered size.

Current NLW evidence supports public-domain commercial reuse of the exact IIIF facsimile. That conclusion is not transferred to U-M encoding markup, Salisbury’s modern edition, Harris’s chapter, or any other layer.
The current U-M NoC-US label and embedded 2003 redistribution ban are preserved together pending written clarification.
